content.text: Monitoring a deployed LLM system is qualitatively different from monitoring a classical ML model. A random-forest classifier produces a single probability score whose drift you can measure with population stability indexes. An LLM produces open-ended text whose quality, safety, and cost all need separate measurement planes — and none of them can be summarised by a single number. Effective LLMOps monitoring requires an instrumentation stack that captures structured telemetry at the request level, derives metrics continuously, and feeds automated evaluators that act as a second tier of quality control in production.
LLM production monitoring spans four distinct dimensions. Treating any one of them as the sole concern produces blind spots that eventually cause incidents.
Directly affects user experience; p99 matters more than mean
LLM inference is billed per token; unbounded prompts burn budget fast
Model updates, prompt drift, and retrieval failures all degrade quality silently
LLM-as-judge scores, thumbs ratings, task success rate
Harmful outputs, PII leaks, and jailbreaks are operational risk
Guardrail trigger rate, content-policy hit rate, PII flag rate

Every LLM request should emit a structured trace containing enough information to reconstruct what happened without replaying the call. The trace is the foundation of all other monitoring: metrics are aggregations over traces, evaluators consume traces, and drift detectors compare trace populations.

import time, uuid, json, logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from typing import Optional

logger = logging.getLogger("llmops.trace")

@dataclass
class LLMTrace:
    trace_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    model: str = ""
    stage: str = "production"           # production | shadow | canary
    prompt_tokens: int = 0
    completion_tokens: int = 0
    ttft_ms: float = 0.0               # time-to-first-token
    total_latency_ms: float = 0.0
    input_guardrail_hit: bool = False
    output_guardrail_hit: bool = False
    retrieval_doc_count: int = 0        # 0 if no RAG
    judge_score: Optional[float] = None # filled async
    user_feedback: Optional[int] = None # +1 / -1 / None
    error: Optional[str] = None

    # runtime state, excluded from serialisation
    _start_ns: int = field(default_factory=time.time_ns, repr=False, compare=False)
    _first_token_ns: int = field(default=0, repr=False, compare=False)

    def record_first_token(self):
        self._first_token_ns = time.time_ns()
        self.ttft_ms = (self._first_token_ns - self._start_ns) / 1_000_000

    def finalise(self):
        self.total_latency_ms = (time.time_ns() - self._start_ns) / 1_000_000

    def emit(self):
        payload = {k: v for k, v in asdict(self).items()
                   if not k.startswith("_")}
        logger.info(json.dumps(payload))
field lets you route canary and shadow traffic through the same telemetry pipeline as production traffic, enabling direct A/B comparisons in your dashboards without separate infrastructure.
Key Metrics and How to Derive Them

Metrics are aggregations emitted by the trace collector into a time-series database (Prometheus, OpenTelemetry Collector, or a cloud-native equivalent). Below are the essential metric families with their recommended histogram buckets and alert thresholds.

from prometheus_client import Histogram, Counter, Gauge

TTFT = Histogram(
    "llm_ttft_ms",
    "Time to first token in milliseconds",
    buckets=[50, 100, 200, 400, 800, 1500, 3000, 6000],
    labelnames=["model", "stage"],
)
TOTAL_LATENCY = Histogram(
    "llm_request_latency_ms",
    "End-to-end request latency in milliseconds",
    buckets=[200, 500, 1000, 2000, 5000, 10000, 30000],
    labelnames=["model", "stage"],
)
REQUEST_ERRORS = Counter(
    "llm_request_errors_total",
    "Number of failed LLM requests",
    labelnames=["model", "stage", "error_type"],
)
ACTIVE_REQUESTS = Gauge(
    "llm_active_requests",
    "Currently in-flight LLM requests",
    labelnames=["model"],
)

def record_trace_metrics(trace: LLMTrace):
    labels = {"model": trace.model, "stage": trace.stage}
    TTFT.labels(**labels).observe(trace.ttft_ms)
    TOTAL_LATENCY.labels(**labels).observe(trace.total_latency_ms)
    if trace.error:
        REQUEST_ERRORS.labels(**labels, error_type=trace.error).inc()

from prometheus_client import Counter as PCounter

TOKEN_USAGE = PCounter(
    "llm_tokens_total",
    "Cumulative tokens consumed",
    labelnames=["model", "stage", "token_type"],   # token_type: prompt | completion
)
COST_USD = PCounter(
    "llm_cost_usd_total",
    "Cumulative cost in USD",
    labelnames=["model", "stage"],
)

# Per-model pricing (USD per 1 M tokens, prompt / completion)
PRICING = {
    "gpt-4o":           (5.00, 15.00),
    "gpt-4o-mini":      (0.15,  0.60),
    "claude-sonnet-4-6": (3.00, 15.00),
}

def record_cost_metrics(trace: LLMTrace):
    labels = {"model": trace.model, "stage": trace.stage}
    TOKEN_USAGE.labels(**labels, token_type="prompt").inc(trace.prompt_tokens)
    TOKEN_USAGE.labels(**labels, token_type="completion").inc(trace.completion_tokens)
    price_in, price_out = PRICING.get(trace.model, (0, 0))
    cost = (trace.prompt_tokens * price_in + trace.completion_tokens * price_out) / 1_000_000
    COST_USD.labels(**labels).inc(cost)
Latency and cost metrics tell you
the system behaves; they say nothing about

the model actually produced. Online evaluation samples a fraction of production traces and scores them asynchronously using a judge model. The judge's score becomes a metric that feeds the same dashboard as latency data.

import asyncio, re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

JUDGE_SYSTEM = """You are a strict evaluator. Score the assistant reply on a 1-5 scale.
1 = wrong or harmful, 3 = acceptable but mediocre, 5 = accurate and well-written.
Reply with a single JSON object: {"score": <1-5>, "reason": "<one sentence>"}"""

@dataclass
class JudgeResult:
    trace_id: str
    score: float          # 1 – 5
    reason: str
    judge_model: str

async def judge_trace(
    trace_id: str,
    user_message: str,
    assistant_reply: str,
    judge_model: str = "gpt-4o-mini",
) -> Optional[JudgeResult]:
    client = AsyncOpenAI()
    prompt = f"User: {user_message}\n\nAssistant: {assistant_reply}"
    try:
        rsp = await client.chat.completions.create(
            model=judge_model,
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": JUDGE_SYSTEM},
                {"role": "user", "content": prompt},
            ],
            temperature=0,
            response_format={"type": "json_object"},
        )
        raw = rsp.choices[0].message.content
        data = __import__("json").loads(raw)
        return JudgeResult(
            trace_id=trace_id,
            score=float(data["score"]),
            reason=data["reason"],
            judge_model=judge_model,
        )
    except Exception as exc:
        return None   # non-fatal; log and skip

Sample at 5–10 % of traffic in steady state; increase the sampling rate automatically when a canary model is being evaluated or when the rolling quality score drops below a threshold. Scoring every request is cost-prohibitive and usually unnecessary — the distribution stabilises quickly.
Classical drift detection compares input feature distributions over time. For LLMs the relevant distribution is the
of incoming prompts: if today's prompts cluster in a region the model was never tested on, quality will silently degrade before the judge score reflects it.

import numpy as np
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass
class DriftReport:
    window_size: int
    reference_centroid: np.ndarray
    current_centroid: np.ndarray
    cosine_distance: float     # 0 = identical, 1 = orthogonal
    alert: bool

def cosine_distance(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
    a_n, b_n = a / (np.linalg.norm(a) + 1e-9), b / (np.linalg.norm(b) + 1e-9)
    return float(1.0 - np.dot(a_n, b_n))

def detect_prompt_drift(
    reference_embeddings: List[np.ndarray],   # baseline window (e.g. first week)
    current_embeddings: List[np.ndarray],     # recent window (e.g. last 24 h)
    alert_threshold: float = 0.15,
) -> DriftReport:
    ref_c  = np.mean(reference_embeddings, axis=0)
    curr_c = np.mean(current_embeddings, axis=0)
    dist   = cosine_distance(ref_c, curr_c)
    return DriftReport(
        window_size=len(current_embeddings),
        reference_centroid=ref_c,
        current_centroid=curr_c,
        cosine_distance=dist,
        alert=dist > alert_threshold,
    )

A cosine distance above 0.15 between the reference centroid and the current centroid is a reliable early-warning signal that warrants investigation. Common causes include a product launch that expands the user base, seasonal topic shifts, and prompt injection campaigns where attackers probe the system with unusual inputs.

attrs.alt: LLMOps monitoring architecture: LLM gateway emits structured traces to a trace store; Prometheus metrics, online eval (LLM-as-judge), and a drift detector each consume the trace stream and feed a Grafana dashboard and PagerDuty alerting pipeline.

content.text: The architecture above separates the critical path (the LLM response path, top row) from all monitoring work (bottom rows). The serving gateway emits a structured trace asynchronously; online eval and drift detection run as background services that read from the trace store. Only the metrics push is in-band, and it is a fire-and-forget write to a local Prometheus push gateway that takes under one millisecond.
Good LLMOps alerts are actionable and have a clear owner. Every alert should answer: what broke, which model/stage, and who fixes it.
Check GPU utilisation; scale replicas or switch tier
Compare against previous model version; trigger rollback if stable
Audit prompt lengths; check for runaway batch jobs
Inspect blocked queries; may signal adversarial campaign
Investigate new query patterns; schedule re-evaluation on new distribution

Keep alert thresholds in version-controlled config, not hard-coded in dashboards. When a model upgrade changes the expected latency baseline, the threshold must be updated atomically with the deployment, or the alert becomes noise and gets ignored.
Monitoring data is most valuable when it closes a loop back into the LLMOps lifecycle. The patterns below turn passive observation into active improvement.
: weekly SQL query on the trace store finds completions where
. These become candidate examples for fine-tuning or few-shot prompt additions.
: every guardrail-triggered input is labelled and appended to the red-teaming corpus for future safety evaluations.
: if the product surface exposes a thumbs-down button, store the user feedback in the trace and replay the query against new model versions in a weekly comparison run.
: aggregate average prompt token count by feature or user segment weekly. Features with p90 prompt > 3 000 tokens are candidates for prompt compression.
: when the drift detector fires, automatically trigger an offline eval run on a sample of the current distribution before the next deployment.
Before declaring a deployment production-ready, verify each item below:
Structured traces emitted on every request with
, model, tokens, latency, and guardrail flags.
Latency histograms (TTFT and end-to-end) ingested into a time-series DB with
Token and USD counters per model and stage.
Online eval sampling wired up; judge scores flowing to metrics.
Drift detector scheduled to run at least daily against a stable baseline window.
Alerts configured for quality drop, cost spike, guardrail surge, and prompt drift.
Dashboard shared with the on-call rotation; runbooks linked from each alert.
Feedback loop established: low-score traces reviewed weekly; findings fed back into eval or fine-tuning.

LLMOps monitoring requires four parallel measurement planes: latency/throughput, token cost, output quality, and safety. Structured per-request traces are the foundation that feeds all four. Online LLM-as-judge evaluation closes the quality gap that metric-only monitoring leaves open. Semantic drift detection provides an early warning before degraded quality appears in judge scores. Closing the loop — routing low-score traces and drift signals back into the improvement pipeline — is what turns monitoring from a passive dashboard into an active quality flywheel.
